channel.title: AGSIST Daily — Morning Agricultural Intelligence Briefing
channel.description: Free daily agricultural market briefing for corn, soybean, and grain producers. Overnight surprises, farmer actions, market analysis — every morning at 5 AM CT.

image.title: AGSIST Daily — Morning Agricultural Intelligence Briefing

item.title: WASDE DAY: WHEAT RUNS 3%, HOGS REVERSE HARD
item.description: The WASDE dropped at 11 AM and the market answered fast. Wheat ran 18.5 cents to $6.30, corn added 8.5 cents to $4.38, and hogs posted a 15.7% session that erased yesterday's 11.8% break almost to the penny. That is not a coincidence: the USDA cut red meat production estimates and trimmed global wheat supply, and the funds that liquidated livestock yesterday came right back in this morning. The weekly thread question from Monday asked whether Crop Progress would confirm pollination-week crop health or build a weather premium into corn. Today the answer is partial: corn is firming on WASDE-driven supply tightening and heat dome positioning, but $4.38 is not yet a weather-premium price. The next leg needs the heat to verify in August crop condition data. USDA's July WASDE at 11 AM CT resets the supply picture as wheat posts its biggest session since spring and hogs erase yesterday's 11.8% drop in a single day. • WASDE Lights the Grain Complex → Unpriced wheat: $6.30 is the highest close since spring. If you have old-crop in storage with carry costs running, the WASDE just gave you a window. Scale sales above $6.30; next resistance is $6.45. • Hogs Reverse Hard, Cattle Split • Hormuz Back on the Tape Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-07-10

item.title: HOGS FALL HARD; EXPORT SALES DECIDE BEANS
item.description: Lean hogs had their biggest single-session drop in months, closing at $85.53, off nearly 12%, and that number is loud enough to drown out everything else this morning. Corn at $4.33 gave back 7¾ cents as profit-taking in beans and a 'buy the rumor, sell the fact' reaction to Wednesday's confirmed China soybean sale dragged the whole complex. The weekly thread's question, does the crop health picture add a weather premium to corn, sits right next to the morning's real make-or-break: USDA export sales hit at 7:30 AM CT, and after three sessions of positioning, the bean market is about to get an answer. Lean hogs dropped nearly 12% in their biggest single-session move in months, while soybeans drift into Thursday's 7:30 AM export sales number as the week's make-or-break moment. • Hogs Break Hard • Beans Hold; Export Sales Decide → Unpriced new-crop soybeans: wait for the 7:30 AM export sales print. Above 400K MT, scale sales toward $12.10. Below 300K MT, reassess storage economics before adding. • Corn Gives Back, Watches Pollination Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-07-09

item.title: CRUDE RUNS 3% AS HORMUZ REIGNITES
item.description: The Hormuz story just reversed course. WTI ran to $74.58, up 3.2% after Iran attacked three commercial vessels off the Omani coast and the U.S. canceled its sanctions waiver overnight, trapping an estimated 63 million barrels of crude at sea. The diplomatic channel that had been quietly unwinding the energy premium since early April is now in question: at least four oil and LNG tankers made U-turns this morning rather than push through the Strait. Grain markets caught a secondary lift on the weather story and short covering in beans, but energy is the headline today, and it's not quiet. Iran attacks commercial vessels, tankers make U-turns, and the Strait of Hormuz premium that was deflating all month snaps back hard. • Hormuz Reignites Energy • Beans Hold Near $12.10 Target → Unpriced new-crop soybeans: hold through Thursday 7:30 AM export sales. Above 400K MT, add sales above $12.10. Below 300K MT, today's gain fades and that's your better entry into storage math. • Corn Grinds, Weather Watches Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-07-08

item.title: BEANS LEAD THE COMPLEX BACK NORTH
item.description: Soybeans had their biggest single-session gain in weeks, nearby up 30¾ cents to $11.81, after USDA confirmed 85.4 million soybean acres Tuesday morning, matching March's estimate and removing the fear of a surprise acreage add. That matters for the weekly thread: Monday's question was whether Crop Progress would confirm pollination-week crop health or add a weather premium, and today's answer is both, corn conditions look solid per field reports out of Michigan and Minnesota, but an approaching Plains heat dome just enough uncertainty to keep a modest weather premium alive in December corn at $4.59. The complex is no longer in Monday's defensive crouch. The question now is whether the funds follow soybeans higher or treat today's move as a one-day cover. Soybeans ran 30 cents on USDA acreage confirmation and heat-dome weather talk; corn and wheat followed, cattle barely moved. • Soybeans: Acreage Clears the Deck → If you have unpriced new-crop soybeans, watch Thursday's export sales. Above 400K MT keeps the move alive toward $12.10; below 300K MT and today's run fades. • Corn: Heat Dome Adds a Little Back • Wheat Follows, Does Not Lead Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-07-07

item.title: CORN BREAKS 4% AGAIN; OATS BREAKDOWN 15%
item.description: Corn is back to $4.25 and the question is no longer whether pollination week carries a weather premium. It carries one, it just isn't being priced. Old-crop nearby fell 17¾ cents to $4.25 today, the same level it closed before the holiday weekend, and December slipped only a quarter to $4.42, which tells you the nearby contract is doing the liquidating while new-crop holds its ground. Today's USDA Crop Progress drops at 3 PM CT, and the market is sitting on its hands waiting for it. Old-crop corn drops 17¾ cents on the first full session back from the holiday weekend, while oats post one of their biggest single-session moves in years, and today's Crop Progress at 3 PM CT resets the story. • Corn: Funds Step Out → If you have unpriced new-crop corn, hold through today's 3 PM Crop Progress. If December breaks below $4.35 on a good-conditions print, reassess storage economics. • Oats: Off the Map • Beans and Wheat: Softer, Not Breaking Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-07-06

item.title: CORN POLLS INTO JULY 4 WEEKEND AT $4.25
item.description: Corn closed Friday at $4.25, down 17¾ cents on the session, and now the Belt has a long weekend to sit with it. The move wasn't noise: nearby corn gave back 4% heading into the most weather-sensitive two weeks of the growing season, and the funds that pushed corn lower into the holiday close will come back Tuesday asking the same question every pollination week asks first: how hot, and for how long. December held better at $4.42, off just 1.3 cents, which tells you the market sold what it could move, not what it believed about the crop. Friday's 4% corn drop sets the table for a weather-driven week ahead with pollination at peak risk. • Corn: Pollination Week Opens → If you have unpriced new-crop corn, hold through Tuesday's Crop Progress and the first post-holiday forecast run. If December breaks below $4.35, that changes the math on storage decisions. • Beans and Wheat: Not Broken, But Soft • Livestock: Pressure Doesn't Quit Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-07-05

item.title: CORN BREAKS 4%; WEEK ENDS ON DEFENSIVE NOTE
item.description: Corn closed Friday at $4.25, down 17¾ cents on the session, the biggest single-day drop in recent memory for a market already carrying pollination anxiety and post-acreage repositioning. The funds spent the holiday-eve session unwinding exposure they no longer wanted to hold over a long weekend. What made Friday different from prior soft days: December corn barely moved, closing $4.42 down just 1¼ cents, which tells you this was nearby-contract liquidation, not a new-crop conviction call. The spread between old crop and new crop is now talking. Coming into next week, the corn market faces a make-or-break stretch: pollination stress either shows up in next Monday's Crop Progress or the weather premium that built this month starts leaking out for good. Nearby corn lost 17¾ cents Friday as the July 4 week closes with grains, livestock, hogs, and dairy all under pressure while energy and metals finish higher. • Corn: Liquidation Into the Long Weekend → If you have unpriced old-crop corn in storage, the nearby-to-December spread is now worth checking against your basis offer. Old-crop basis firmness east of the Mississippi may partially offset the futures drop. • Beans and Wheat: Weaker, Not Broken • Livestock and Dairy: Pressure Holds Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-07-04

item.title: CORN BREAKS 4% ON HOLIDAY EVE; OATS HIT -15%
item.description: Corn closed Thursday at $4.25, down 4.0% in a single session, the biggest one-day drop in months for a crop that is right now, this weekend, pollinating across the Belt. Oats fell 15.5% to $2.86, a move that has no clean catalyst in Thursday's news and looks like fund liquidation in a thin pre-holiday session. Markets are closed today. The question that reopens Monday is whether the heat premium that built all week survived the selloff, or whether someone decided to cash it in before the long weekend. Markets closed Friday for Independence Day; Thursday's close left corn at $4.25, oats in freefall, and the July 4th weekend sitting on top of peak pollination. • Corn Sells Into Pollination • Beans Hold Better Than Corn • Livestock Fades Into Weekend Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-07-03

item.title: HEAT OWNS THE BELT; EXPORT SALES ARRIVE AT 7:30
item.description: Corn closed $4.24 and beans held $11.33, both up on the session, as a heat dome parked over the Heartland kept weather premium alive through the holiday week. Brownfield's midweek weather outlook puts anomalous heat running from the central Belt into early next week, right through the window when corn pollination is most vulnerable. The question that decides the next 20 cents in December corn lands at 7:30 CT this morning: export sales. Renewed talk of China buying U.S. beans circulated Wednesday, but no new sales were announced. Today's print either validates that talk or files it under rumors. Corn and beans stayed green Wednesday on pollination heat, but Thursday morning's export sales print will tell you whether China talk is real or noise. • Grains: Heat Holds the Premium • Beans: China Talk Meets Export Day → If you held unpriced new-crop beans per yesterday's guidance, wait for the 7:30 export sales. Under 300,000 MT and November $11.54 is under pressure. Above 500,000 MT, hold. • Livestock: Hogs Slip, Cattle Marks Time Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-07-02

item.title: ACREAGE VERDICT: CORN HOLDS, BEANS SLIP
item.description: Tuesday's USDA Acreage and Quarterly Stocks reports are in the books, and the market's verdict is measured, not decisive. Corn nearby sits at $4.14, essentially unchanged, while December corn eased to $4.38. The stocks figure came in below what most economists expected to be bearish, and that nuance is the story: the market was braced for a gut-punch on corn acres, didn't get one, and now the next move belongs to the weather. Highs of 90 to 95 degrees across the Corn Belt with high humidity are pushing the 2026 crop through pollination at speed, and whether that heat shows up as yield drag or accelerated development is the question that reprices December corn next. Lower-than-expected corn stocks from Tuesday's USDA data gave bulls a brief read, but November beans gave back 5.5 cents and the heat wave building across the Belt is doing the real work now. • Corn: Heat Takes the Wheel • Beans: November Slips on Acreage Math → Hold unpriced new-crop beans until Thursday export sales. Under 300,000 MT puts November under pressure. • Oats Overnight: Still Searching for a Floor Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-07-01

item.title: ACREAGE DATA LANDS; CORN HOLDS $4.04
item.description: The numbers that have been hanging over this market all week printed at 11 AM CT, and now corn sits at $4.04 with the answer in hand. The setup coming in: analysts expected USDA to confirm north of 95 million corn acres, a figure that, if matched or beaten, removes the last bull argument propping up old-crop nearby. The heat wave gripping the Heartland through the end of the week adds a new variable at the worst possible time. Pollination is approaching across the Belt, and triple-digit temperatures from the southern High Plains northward do not sit well with silking corn. The acreage data tells you how many acres are at risk. The weather tells you what happens to them. USDA's dual 11 AM release answers the acre question; heat wave arrives just as pollination approaches. • Corn: Report Day Verdict • Oats Recover; Soyoil Pulls Back • Cattle Fall; Processing Drag Continues Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-30

item.title: ACREAGE REPORT DAY: GRAINS BREAK, SOYOIL RUNS
item.description: Corn hit $4.13 this morning and the funds aren't waiting for Tuesday's Acreage report to find out if it gets worse. The nearby gave back 10½ cents while December held at $4.42, which tells you exactly where the real fear lives: old-crop is being sold, new-crop is being held. Wheat fell 2.9% to $5.78 with no weather premium to defend it, and oats had another session that defies easy explanation, down 15% for the second time in a week. The Acreage report at 11 AM CT tomorrow will either validate every one of these moves or reverse them. Until then, the market has already voted. Corn falls 2.5% and wheat drops 2.9% heading into Tuesday's USDA Acreage report, while soybean oil runs nearly 7% on biofuel demand and the Hormuz premium reignites. • Corn Breaks Into the Report → If you have unpriced old-crop corn in storage and local basis is firm today, move bushels before Tuesday's open. The Acreage report removes the uncertainty in one direction or the other. • Wheat Falls Hard, No Floor Visible • Soyoil Runs; Complex Splits Wide Open → Producers with unpriced soybeans: the November contract at $11.56 is 30 cents over nearby, which is carry, not a signal to sell new-crop early. Wait for Tuesday's Acreage number before making a new-crop decision. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-29

item.title: ACREAGE REPORT TUESDAY; CORN BREAKS HARD INTO IT
item.description: Corn dropped 2.5% on Friday to $4.13, and December new crop barely budged at $4.42, a spread that tells you the market is pricing weather risk selectively and not yet believing the heat dome translates into a yield problem. Tuesday's USDA Acreage Report at 11 AM CT lands before that argument gets settled. The number that comes back on corn acres will either confirm the tight supply picture that should be holding this market above $4 or hand the funds a reason to sell harder into an already-weakened chart. Friday's close puts corn at $4.13 with a heat dome building and the season's most important USDA print two days out. • CORN: $4.13 AND A TUESDAY GUN → If you have unpriced old-crop corn in storage and local basis is firm, price before Tuesday open. If you are holding new-crop, wait for the acreage number before making a decision. • BEANS AND WHEAT: SPLIT STORY INTO ACREAGE • CATTLE: BOXED BEEF BROKE THE WEEK Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-28

item.title: CORN AND WHEAT CLOSE WEAK; SOYOIL RUNS HARD
item.description: Corn finished Friday at $4.13, down two and a half percent on the session, and the week ends with old-crop corn sitting at its lowest 52-week percentile since late winter. Wheat gave back nearly three percent to close at $5.78. The story that cuts against the grain selloff: soybean oil ran almost seven percent higher to $71.30, the strongest close in the soy complex all week, and meal held. That split tells you the market is repricing biofuel demand, not abandoning oilseeds. Tuesday's USDA crop reports land into a crop that is days away from the corn pollination window, and the heat dome already building across the central Belt means nobody gets to be patient much longer. A rough Friday for grains sets up a make-or-break week: Tuesday's USDA planting and acreage data lands while a heat dome builds over the Belt. • GRAINS CLOSE WEEK UNDER PRESSURE → Producers with unpriced old-crop corn: if local basis is firm, this is not the week to wait. The nearby contract is at $4.13 and the heat premium for new crop has not yet shown up in the December contract at $4.42. Price old-crop on firm basis days before Tuesday lands. • SOYOIL RUNS; COMPLEX SPLITS • OATS BREAKDOWN CONTINUES → If you have unpriced oats in storage, check basis today. The futures contract is near 52-week lows and there is no visible floor in the price action. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-27

item.title: ACREAGE REPORT TUESDAY; OATS HIT HARD AGAIN
item.description: The 2026 planting season, behind the average through May before catching up between rain systems, is about to face its next test: a heat dome building from the Plains to the Atlantic Coast by Sunday, and a USDA Acreage report dropping Tuesday at 11 AM CT that could reprice every contract in the complex. Corn closed $4.14, up a fraction, and November beans held $11.50, up less than a dime. Neither number is the story today. The story is that the market spent Friday coiling ahead of data that actually matters, and the heat forecast is the first weather catalyst in weeks that has a real claim on new-crop bushels. Corn and beans barely moved Friday, but a 13% oats drop and incoming Heartland heat reset the story heading into a data-heavy week. • GRAINS MARK TIME BEFORE DATA • OATS FALL HARD AGAIN → If you have unpriced oats in storage, evaluate today's basis against your cost of carry. The fund exit may not be finished. • CATTLE HOLD; MILK FALLS HARD Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-26

item.title: EXPORT SALES REPORT DUE; CORN STABILIZES AFTER 6% BREAK
item.description: Corn climbed back 2 cents to $4.07 Thursday morning, a modest recovery after Wednesday's continuation of the prior session's hard break, and the market is essentially holding its breath until USDA's weekly export sales land at 7:30 AM CT. Traders have been optimistic about Chinese soybean demand all week without a single announced sale to back it up, so today's report is either confirmation or a cold shower. The 2026 planting season, behind the average through May before catching up between rain systems, put pollination-window weather front and center now, and one number in the export report will reset the whole narrative. Nearby corn adds 2 cents as USDA weekly export sales hit the wire at 7:30 AM CT, with China soybean demand the number traders are watching. • CORN STEADIES, EXPORT SALES DECIDE • BEANS POSITION AHEAD OF REPORT → If you have unpriced new-crop beans, hold through 7:30 AM CT. If the export number confirms China buying above 500K MT, November at $11.38 has room to run. If it misses, price into any rally. • OATS BREAK HARD OVERNIGHT Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-25

item.title: CORN NEARBY BREAKS 6%; OLD-CROP CARRY INVERTS
item.description: Nearby corn dropped 27 cents to $4.11 while Dec '26 barely twitched, closing at $4.38. That 27-cent spread between the nearby and new crop is the market telling you storage is not being rewarded right now; it is being penalized. Heat and humidity are moving into the Heartland over the next 6-to-10 days per this morning's weather outlook, and pollination is approaching. The question this week was whether Crop Progress data removed the last weather premium from corn. Today's break in the nearby says the premium was already gone, but the calendar is about to test that assumption. Nearby corn fell hard while Dec '26 barely moved, blowing the carry structure wide open and leaving producers with a storage decision to make before pollination weather takes over the narrative. • NEARBY CORN BREAKS HARD → Producers with on-farm grain: check local basis today. If basis is firm, the futures break has narrowed your window. Evaluate before the heat forecast gets priced further into the new-crop contract. • BEANS AND WHEAT HOLD STEADY • CATTLE WAIT ON DIRECT TRADE Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-24

item.title: WHEAT LEADS LOWER; FARM BILL DRAFT DROPS
item.description: Wheat did the most damage today, off nearly 10 cents to $5.88, while corn eased another 2.5 cents to $4.10 and soybeans barely moved. The real news wasn't on the board: Senate Ag Committee Chairman Boozman dropped the GOP farm bill discussion draft this afternoon, the Agricultural Act of 2026, and producers need to read it before the commentary class does. Meanwhile, Monday's Crop Progress held national corn condition steady, development weather looks mostly favorable, and the crop-progress thread from this week's question is starting to answer itself: no weather premium is being added, and the market is confirming it session by session. Chicago wheat gave back nearly 10 cents Tuesday as Senate Ag released the Agricultural Act of 2026 and cattle waited on direct trade. • WHEAT LEADS THE COMPLEX LOWER • CATTLE WAIT; DIRECT TRADE SETS THE PRICE • FARM BILL DRAFT HITS THE WIRE Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-23

item.title: CORN FALLS 7%; CROP PROGRESS DUE AT 3 PM
item.description: Corn fell hard Monday, nearby settling at $4.12, a 7% drop that puts it at the worst close of its recent range and demands an explanation before you do anything else. The December contract only eased 3 cents to $4.40, which tells you this is old-crop liquidation, not a new-crop conviction call. USDA Crop Progress drops at 3 PM CT; if the Belt is running ahead of pace and soil moisture stays at 150% of normal, today's move will look like a setup, not a bottom. Nearby corn posted its biggest single-session drop in months; USDA Crop Progress at 3 PM CT will either explain it or deepen the question. • OLD-CROP CORN BREAKS HARD → Producers with old-crop bushels in storage: this is a window-closing move. If basis is still firm in your area, evaluate before the 3 PM report resets the narrative. • CATTLE FIRMS, PROCESSING STILL CONSTRAINED • CRUDE EASES, HORMUZ PREMIUM CONTINUES TO DEFLATE Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-22

item.title: BEANS AND WHEAT BROKE FRIDAY; CATTLE HELD
item.description: Friday's close told two different stories. Beans at $11.23 nearby and wheat at $6.06 both gave back real ground heading into the weekend, the kind of selling that happens when producers and funds don't want to carry risk through a three-day stretch. Live cattle at $254.80 held and then some, up 2.3% on the session, the Cargill Fort Morgan lockout still tightening the available processing chain enough to support fed cattle values even as feeder cattle at $366.60 barely moved. The week ahead has a specific make-or-break: Monday's Crop Progress at 3 PM CT will tell you whether the cool, wet pattern rolling back into the Heartland is helping crops or starting to crowd the pollination window. That number will move the grain markets more than anything else on the calendar. Soybeans lost 24 cents and wheat gave back 14 cents to close the week; live cattle ran to $254.80 while the complex digests the Cargill lockout and a cool, wet forecast sets up the week. • GRAINS BREAK INTO THE WEEKEND • CATTLE HOLDS THE LINE • CRUDE FIRMS, METALS FALL HARD Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-21

item.title: GRAINS BREAK, CATTLE HOLDS, WEEK RESET AHEAD
item.description: Soybeans gave back $0.24¾ Friday to close at $11.23, wheat dropped 14 cents to $6.06, and corn slipped to $4.17 into a three-day Juneteenth weekend with nobody left to defend them. The grains went out soft not because a fresh bear catalyst showed up, but because the calendar did: traders don't want to carry risk over a long weekend with corn pollination approaching and the weather pattern turning cooler and wetter across the Belt. Cattle told a different story. Live cattle ran to $254.80, up 2.3% on Friday, off the Cattle on Feed data delivered Thursday, while feeders barely moved at $366.60. Two markets, two entirely different reads on the same week. Friday's close handed grains their worst day of the week; cattle split the difference into the three-day weekend. • GRAINS BREAK INTO THE WEEKEND • CATTLE SPLITS THE DIFFERENCE • CRUDE FIRMS, METALS FALL HARD Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-20

item.title: CATTLE ON FEED DELIVERS; GRAINS BREAK INTO HOLIDAY
item.description: The Cattle on Feed report at 2:00 PM CT delivered the number the market was waiting for all week: May placements down 10% year-over-year, tight enough to justify today's move. Live cattle ran to $254.80, biggest single-session gain in several weeks, while the rest of the complex sorted itself out around that story. Soybeans told the other half of Friday's tale, nearby falling 24¾ cents to $11.23 as Thursday's export sales came in soft and the soybean oil break that started Wednesday kept pulling the complex lower. The Belt caught up, the crop looks fine, and the market is heading into a three-day weekend with cattle bulls feeling vindicated and grain sellers in charge. Placements dropped 10% from last year, live cattle ran 2.3% higher, and soybeans lost 25 cents as export demand disappointed heading into the Juneteenth weekend. • CATTLE ON FEED DELIVERS • BEANS BREAK, WHEAT FOLLOWS • CRUDE FIRMS, METALS DROP Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-19

item.title: SOYBEAN OIL BREAKS HARD, EXPORT SALES DUE 7:30
item.description: Soybean oil fell hard Thursday, losing 6.7% to close at $66.08, its biggest single-session drop in months, splitting the oilseed complex right before the USDA weekly export sales hit at 7:30 AM CT. Soybeans nearby followed only modestly lower at $11.41, off 6½ cents, because Wednesday's USDA flash sale of 13.7 million bushels to unknown destinations is still sitting in the market's memory. The export number this morning decides whether that flash sale was a one-off or the front edge of something bigger. Watch the 7:30 print. A 6.7% drop in soybean oil splits the oilseed complex ahead of Thursday's weekly export sales report, with cattle flat and the grain board drifting. • SOYBEAN OIL BREAKS → Old-crop soybean sellers: the flash sale gave nearby beans a cushion this week. If today's export total tops 400K MT, that cushion firms. Under 300K MT, the oil break starts pulling beans with it. • GRAINS DRIFT, EXPORTS LOOM • CATTLE MARKS TIME Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-18

item.title: CATTLE RUNS HARD, WHEAT HOLDS, CORN FIRMS
item.description: Live cattle at $249.12 is up 2.4% on the session, the biggest move in the complex in weeks, and it happened the day after the Cargill Fort Morgan lockout looked like it had broken the chart. That is the market telling you something: the buyer is not gone, the processing constraint is not the whole story, and screwworm case counts climbing to 12 are starting to price supply tightness the lockout alone couldn't explain. Meanwhile, wheat at $6.14 is holding most of Monday's 4% run, and a climatologist at the Midwestern Regional Climate Center is flagging another severe weather round bearing down on the Eastern Corn Belt. The weekly thread question is still live: did the Belt catch up to seasonal pace, and now the follow-on question is whether it matters if a tornado outbreak erases the progress. Live cattle's biggest single-session gain in weeks leads a broadly green close, while another ECB severe weather threat keeps grain bulls interested. • CATTLE RUNS BACK • WHEAT HOLDS, CORN FIRMS • SOYBEANS STEADY Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-17

item.title: WHEAT RUNS 4%, CRUDE DROPS HARD ON IRAN DEAL
item.description: Wheat is the story today. Chicago wheat closed at $6.08, up 24½ cents, its biggest day in months, while crude fell hard to $75.80 on news that U.S.-Iran diplomatic channels are producing real results and Middle East supply is coming back online. The weekly thread question was whether USDA Crop Progress would confirm the Belt caught up to seasonal pace; Monday's report answered that clearly, and now the market is repricing around a different concern: Iowa corn rated good-to-excellent dropped from 84% to 79% after last week's severe weather, and a cool, wet pattern is moving back into the Heartland. The question is no longer whether the Belt planted on time. It's whether the crop that went in the ground stays healthy. Chicago wheat had its biggest single-session gain in months while WTI crude fell sharply 6.1% as U.S.-Iran diplomatic progress unwinds the Middle East supply premium. • WHEAT LEADS THE COMPLEX • CATTLE RECOVERS, BOTTLENECK HOLDS • CRUDE DROPS HARD, INPUTS SHIFT → If you haven't locked fall diesel, the window from crude's move may be short; get a quote this week. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-16

item.title: SOYBEANS LEAD HIGHER, CATTLE SLIPS ON SCREWWORM RESTRICTIONS
item.description: Soybeans closed $11.32, up 19 cents and leading the complex higher as crude oil's retreat signals easing input costs from the Strait of Hormuz reopening. But the grain rally couldn't lift cattle, which dropped 0.6% to $241.18 as Canada and Mexico imposed import restrictions following screwworm detection in the U.S. The divergence tells the story: grain fundamentals improving while livestock faces structural headwinds that won't resolve with a phone call. Beans gain nearly 2% as crude eases on Hormuz progress while livestock faces trade disruption. • SOYBEANS LEAD COMPLEX HIGHER • CATTLE DROPS ON TRADE RESTRICTIONS • CORN STEADIES BEFORE KEY WEEK Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-15

item.title: CATTLE HOLDS GAINS, CORN STEADIES AHEAD OF KEY WEEK
item.description: Cattle closed Friday at $249.88, up 3% for the week, and the momentum looks sticky heading into Monday. The Cargill Fort Morgan lockout that hammered feeders three weeks ago is showing resolution signs, and the processing chain is catching up to the backlog. Corn held $4.13 after finding its footing above contract lows, but the real test comes Tuesday when USDA drops Crop Progress with pollination season approaching. Live cattle closed Friday at $249.88, up 3% for the week as processing disruptions ease. • CATTLE COMPLEX HOLDS MOMENTUM • CORN STEADIES BEFORE TUESDAY TEST • ENERGY EASES ON HORMUZ PROGRESS Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-14

item.title: CATTLE BOUNCES BACK, GRAINS FIND FOOTING AFTER WEEK OF SLIDES
item.description: Live cattle closed Friday at $249.88, up 3% in the session and marking the sharpest single-day gain since early May. The bounce came as processing capacity concerns that hammered the complex earlier this week showed signs of easing. Corn held $4.13, steady to firm despite reports of rootless syndrome spreading through Indiana and eastern Illinois. The week that started with slides across both complexes ended with cattle finding its legs and grains grinding toward what might finally be a floor. Live cattle rallied 3% Friday as processing concerns ease, corn steadied despite rootless syndrome spreading in Belt. • CATTLE COMPLEX BOUNCES HARD • CORN STEADIES DESPITE DISEASE PRESSURE • SOYBEANS SOFTEN ON FUND SELLING Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-13

item.title: GRAINS FIND BOTTOM, LIVESTOCK SLIPS ON USDA SUPPLY VIEW
item.description: Corn closed $4.13, up half a cent and looking like it finally found the floor the funds have been hunting for three weeks. December corn at $4.41 tells the story better: new-crop's firming while old-crop stays defensive, the calendar spread working exactly like it should when planting's done and the weather hasn't turned. The grain complex answered this week's question with a definitive yes, breaking the five-day slide on fund rotation and fresh energy in the ethanol story. Corn leads grain bounce while cattle complex falls on milk production outlook. • CORN LEADS GRAINS HIGHER • CATTLE COMPLEX FALLS ON USDA OUTLOOK • OATS FALL ON OVERNIGHT SELLING Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-12

item.title: GRAINS SLIP AHEAD OF WASDE, CATTLE HOLD GAINS
item.description: Corn closed $4.15, down 4 cents and leading the grain complex lower ahead of this morning's WASDE report at 11:00 AM CT. The funds are positioning defensive with managed money net long still above 100,000 contracts, but yesterday's Brazil CONAB estimate adding 0.2% to their corn outlook removes any supply tightness narrative. Cattle held $242.07, up a dime, as screwworm confirmation paradoxically steadied the complex by removing uncertainty even as local management costs rise. Corn leads complex lower while livestock splits on screwworm clarity. • CORN LEADS GRAINS LOWER • CATTLE STEADY ON SCREWWORM CLARITY • MILK FALLS SHARPLY ON OVERNIGHT SURPRISE Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-06-11
